senses.definition: an old or dialect word for a book or notebook used for recording scorers or notes, now rare or obsolete
examples: The archaic term scorbuch appears in old texts referring to a ledger or notebook.
Scholars cite the scorbuch when discussing medieval accounting practices.

senses.definition: a historical term for scurvy, a disease caused by vitamin C deficiency
examples: Historical doctors described scorbute as a dangerous condition in sailors.
Dietary improvement helped prevent scorbute in the 18th century.

senses.definition: relating to or affected by scurvy; describing symptoms or conditions typical of scurvy
examples: The scorbutic gums were a telltale sign to sailors.
A scorbutic diet contributed to poor health.

senses.definition: relating to scurvy or its symptoms; used chiefly in historical contexts
examples: Scorbutical symptoms appeared among sailors in long voyages.
They discussed scorbutical conditions in old medical texts.

senses.definition: in a scorbutic manner; in a way relating to or resembling scurvy
examples: He ate scorbutically processed foods, leading to health concerns.
The diary described scorbutically affected sailors.

senses.definition: to treat, cure, or cause to become scorbutic; to produce scorbutic symptoms
examples: Historical texts describe attempts to scorbutize sailors with poor diets.
Poor nutrition scorbutizes some populations.

senses.definition: an obsolete or medical term referring to scurvy
examples: Treatises of the era mention scorbuthus as a dangerous disease.
Scorbutus was a major problem before citrus was understood.

senses.definition: to grit, char, or burn superficially; to creosote or scorch lightly
examples: The sun scorce the leaves, leaving a brown edge.
Don't scorce the paint; it will dull the surface.

senses.definition: to burn the surface of something with very high heat; to injure with heat
examples: The iron scorched the fabric.
The sun scorched the dry grass.

senses.definition: a mark or patch burned onto a surface by heat
examples: There was a scorch on the sleeve.
The scorch left a blackened line.

senses.definition: burned or singed on the surface; depleted or worn out by heat
examples: Scorched earth policy caused widespread destruction.
The scorched toast smelled burnt.

senses.definition: a person or thing that scorches; a very hot or intense thing or moment
examples: The heatwave was a real scorcher.
That new oven is a kitchen scorcher.

senses.definition: plural of scorcher; things or people that scorch; plural indicating multiple instances of intense heat
examples: The scorcheres ruined many crops.
Two scorcher days followed by a storm.

senses.definition: marks left by burning surfaces; or the act of scorching
examples: There are scorch marks on the table.
The scorches on the fabric are visible.

senses.definition: very hot or intense; causing burning heat
examples: The scorching sun; a scorching performance.

senses.definition: in a highly hot or intense manner
examples: The heatwave raged scorchingly across the city.
The performance stretched scorchingly long.

senses.definition: the quality of being scorchingly hot or intense
examples: The scorchingness of the day surprised everyone.
Her description captured the scorchingness of the heat.

senses.definition: plural of scorchingness
examples: We recorded several scorchingnesses during the heatwave.
The report noted multiple scorchingsnesses.

senses.definition: burn marks or instances of scorching; intense heat events
examples: There were several scorchings on the fence.
The scorchings on the road shimmered in the heat.

senses.definition: resistant to scorching; able to withstand high heat without burning or damage
examples: The scorchproof coating protected the metal.
Choose scorchproof fabrics for outdoor gear.

senses.definition: plural of scorch; a misspelling or dialect form; generally not a standard form
examples: A few scorchs appeared in the manuscript.
These scorchs indicate data loss.

senses.definition: a musical term indicating a skipped or broken note, especially in stringed instruments; also a musical abbreviation
examples: The piece includes a scordato passage that adds tension.
A scordato figure can sound deliberately rough.

senses.definition: an alteration of the normal tuning of a stringed instrument (used in some violins, violas, and guitars) to produce special sounds
examples: The violinist used scordatura to create unusual timbres.
Scordatura allows unique resonances in performance.

senses.definition: plural of scordatura; multiple instances of altered tunings in stringed instruments
examples: The luthier demonstrated several scordaturas.
Different scordaturas produce varied overtones.

senses.definition: a general term for scordatura; the practice of retuning strings for special effects
examples: Scordature requires careful preparation and skill.
The composer used scordature for eerie sounds.

senses.definition: a small bitter herb used in leavening or in certain historical medicines; also a term in alchemical or herbal contexts
examples: The archaic recipe called for scordium in small amounts.
Herbalists wrote of scordium as a bitter herb.
